tweets.text:
Kimi K3 2.8T is so large that it will not fit on a single NVIDIA DGX B200, even at FP4. A GB300 NVL72, B300, or MI355X system is required, as each GPU has 288 GB of memory.

One optimization that could make Kimi K3 fit on B200 is to gang multiple nodes together and use a technique called WideEP. The issue is that B200 has only 400 Gbit/s of bandwidth between nodes, whereas NVL72 has 18× higher inter-node bandwidth.

Everyone has takes on SMIC. We have die shots.

Introducing STEEL — SemiAnalysis Teardown Engineering & Evaluation Lab. From the box to the bare die: materials, process, integration, every piece of the puzzle.

They want the impossible? We'll make it possible. They want cutting edge? We're on the edge.
Find out more about the STEEL team and all the growth the industry can soon see: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ntiSw-86IN8&feature=youtu.be

Jensen is in Tokyo this week, where he met with executives from Sega Corporation to commemorate their respective companies' special relationship. In the mid-90s, Nvidia was contracted to design the GPU for the Sega Dreamcast console, but Nvidia couldn't deliver on the contract. This left Nvidia with no revenue and in a precarious position as a young startup. Despite this, Sega decided to give Nvidia a lifeline with a $5 million investment, keeping Nvidia afloat. When the history books are written on how we reached AGI, let's not forget how Sonic the Hedgehog helped us speed run the singularity.
Scale up CPO on the GPU is not ramping. That's what we said is delayed.
We said kyber failed. Not Rubin. There is a difference lol
We've been very public about helios rack shipments being delayed out of this year, yes? AMD denied those too, but we are still right
I said kyber is delayed not rubin silly. But rubin is delayed a couple months vs originaly timeline

AMD Helios. NVIDIA Vera Rubin. Double-wide racks. Liquid cooling. And why storage just became the main event. @dylan522p sat down with @Supermicro_SMCI Chief Business Officer Vik Malyala for a wide-ranging conversation. Full interview below.  (1/3)

A few implications we work through in the piece:

The labs can cut sticker prices on tokens, hold or expand gross margin, and still take share. That has been the actual playbook for the Opus pricing reset, not a margin sacrifice.

Memory vendors (SanDisk, Western Digital, Seagate, Micron) ran 200%+ in 2025 because they captured the throughput-scaling upside without the labs being in a position to push back.

Nvidia has more pricing power than its using, especially on SOCAMM into Vera Rubin, and we lay out where on the curve they sit and why they haven't pulled the lever yet. (3/4)

The rough framework we use, and the one the article works through end-to-end, is cost-based floor vs value-based ceiling. On VR NVL72 those numbers are roughly $4.92/hr/GPU and $12.25/hr/GPU. Theres a lot of headroom in that range, and where the market settles is the actual question for the next two years. (4/4)

Scale up CPO on GPU is delayed as we said. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
Not my job to care about stocks.
My job is to track the supply chain accurately

Micron did fail, and then later Nvidia brought down the pin speed targets they had for Rubin as a whole, which enabled the ecosystem to ship to them. If you read the actual note instead of regurgitated nonsense you'd know that.

The bottom line is this: even though we expect co-packaged optics (CPO) to eventually be the go-to bandwidth scaling solution for optics, scale-up CPO will have to work through the integration, yield, and ecosystem maturity curve before it is ready for volume manufacturing and adoption ahead of the 2029 volume adoption timeline that we are anticipating. In the meantime, coherent-lite technology, which has better signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) tolerance and scales bandwidth using a higher-order modulation scheme (DP-16QAM) than traditional IM-DD PAM4 transceivers, is one of the many pragmatic solutions for optical bandwidth scaling, particularly in the high-bandwidth scale-up domain. That said, coherent-lite carries several structural advantages beyond the Google ICI scale-up use case — 2-20km reach versus IM-DD's sub-2km ceiling, fewer lasers per bit of bandwidth, and reduced fiber count — that make it a credible deployment option across other intra-datacenter interconnect tiers as well. (2/2)

There can be no Neoouds without the Trinity.

Excited to announce the SemiAnalysis Teardown Engineering & Evaluation Lab (STEEL)
We have been building a state-of-the-art teardown lab in Oregon with $10s of millions Capex being spent capable of analyzing the world's most advanced and important chips and process technologies over the last year and half.
We have already generated revenue on advanced datacenter chip teardowns.
This is a bit of inconvenient timing for TechInsights as they are private equity owned and currently being sold while having enjoyed virtually no credible competition for decades. This has led to TechInsights underinvesting in CAPEX.
SemiAnalysis exceeds TechInsights in revenue despite no venture or private equity ownership and being founded only 6 years ago.
Because we have no external investors and are founder led, we move faster, build faster, and we can release client chip teardowns for free regularly, while focusing on datacenter for our major clients.
STEEL has a state of the art fleet of tools.
We are less than a year and half into the journey here, but our goal is to be number 1 just like we are in AI, Datacenter, and Semiconductor market data, consulting, and technically analysis.
Our largest customers which, include all of the hyperscalers and world's largest semiconductor companies, are excited for us to shake up the market.
Clients of SemiAnalysis Memory and Accelerator model knew about Rubin Ultra 16 Hi to 12 Hi cut in March :)

In RNG, the ToRs are not directly connected to each other, but via shuffle boxes, which are passive optical components. On the standard design, the datacenter is composed of 10 rooms, with 96 ToRs and 192 shuffle boxes per room, and 64 servers per ToR (6,144 total). (5/7)

Overall, with the reduction in layers, Amazon is claiming up to 45% cost saving compared to fat trees on a similar oversubscription ratio. As of now, RNG is the first network based on random graphs concept deployed at scale. Amazon has currently deployed only 2 version of RNG: "server mesh" that connects servers to ToRs, and "edge mesh" that connects server mesh to the peering datacenters. (6/7)
I'm told we're idiots for forecasting 100,000 Tbps or 50 earths worth of bandwidth over the next 5 years.
